pages.text: Evaluation of high-performance porous concrete properties M. Aamer Raﬁque Bhutta a, K. Tsuruta b, J. Mirza c,⇑ a Faculty of Civil Engineering, University Teknologi Malaysia, Malaysia b Materras Oume Concrete Industry, Ltd., Japan c Department of Robotics and Civil, Research Institute of Hydro-Québec, Varennes, Québec, Canada J3X 1S1 article info Article history: Received 4 July 2011 Received in revised form 1 December 2011 Accepted 4 December 2011 Available online 20 January 2012 Keywords: High performance porous concrete Self-compaction Permeability Compressive strength Flexural strength Strength development rate Total void ratio abstract The aim of this laboratory study was to evaluate the properties of high performance porous concrete. It required no special vibration equipment and curing. The optimum mixture proportions were used in the preparation of high performance porous concretes containing three sizes of coarse aggregates with appropriate amount of high water-reducing and thickening (cohesive) agents. Tests carried out on this concrete were: slump, slump-ﬂow, void ratio, and coefﬁcient of permeability, compressive and ﬂexural strengths, and strength development rate. Furthermore, a test was proposed to determine the effects of high water-reducing and thickening (cohesive) agents on self-compaction of high performance porous concrete. It was meant to evaluate its hardened properties from the viewpoint of practical application. Its strength development rate was also examined at curing age of 1, 3, 7, 14 and 28 days at 20 /C176C and 60% relative humidity (R. H.). Consequently, high performance porous concrete exhibited good workability and cohesiveness with no segregation or bleeding, and developed high strength compared to conven- tional porous concrete. The results of proposed self-compaction test for this porous concrete also showed good workability and cohesiveness without any special compaction or vibration. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Porous concrete was developed as an environmentally friendly material in Japan in the 1980s. Since then it has been widely used in various applications in Japan, USA and Europe because of its multiple environmental beneﬁts: controlling storm water runoff, restoring groundwater supplies, and reducing water and soil pollu- tion [1–6]. Due to the water-permeating, water-draining, and water retaining performances of the porous concrete, it has been utilized in road pavements, sidewalks, parks, and building exteri- ors. This concrete has also been used for plant bedding and perme- able rainwater retention facilities, such as permeable trenches, permeable gullies and permeable gutters [7–9]. In general, a gap- graded conventional porous concrete (CPC or ‘‘no ﬁnes’’ concrete, i.e., where the ﬁne aggregate is omitted entirely) is obtained by using a uniform size of coarse aggregate at low water–cement ratio (W/C). However, this CPC showed poor workability (<30%), needed vibration equipment for proper compaction and curing for the pro- duction of precast products, and for drainage pavement application [10]. This research study tried to develop and evaluate the properties of high performance porous concrete (HPPC) in the laboratory. The purpose was to examine the applicability of HPPC in terms of its practical application in place of CPC. A test was also developed to determine the effect of the thickening (cohesive) and high water- reducing agents (SP) on self-compaction

only takes place in the presence of sufﬁcient water. Since porous concrete has low W/C, therefore, both the CPC and HPPC specimens were placed at 20 ± 1 /C176C and 100% R. H. for 1-d and then subjected to 20 ± 1 /C176C and 60% R. H. for 27-d. To obtain mean value, three identical specimens were prepared for each type of concretes and for each test. HPPC exhibited good workability and cohesiveness to ﬁll the spaces of almost any size and shape without segregation or bleeding; required no special vibration equipment and curing, and developed high strength [11,12]. 2.3. Tests The tests carried out on the specimens were: slump and slump-ﬂow, total void ratio ( u10 /C2 20 cm), compressive strength ( u10 /C2 20 cm) according to JIS A 1108, ﬂexural strength (10 /C2 10 /C2 40 cm) according to JIS A 1106, co-efﬁcient of perme- ability ( u10 /C2 20 cm) and strength development rate. The strength development rate was tested at curing period of 1, 3, 7, 14 and 28 days. As mentioned earlier, a test method was also developed to determine the effect of SP and the thickening (cohesive) agents on self-compaction of HPPC and its hardened properties by keep- ing practical application in mind. As a result, the porous concrete exhibited good workability and cohesiveness with no segregation or bleeding and provided high strength compared to CPC. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Slump and slump-ﬂow To maintain the consistency of SP and thickening agent in HPPC mixtures, slump and slump-ﬂow tests were conducted in accor- dance with JIS A 1101 (Method of Test for Slump of concrete) and JIS A 1150 (Method of Test for slump-ﬂow of concrete), respec- tively. These tests help a lot in ﬁeld applications of HPPC. On the other hand, these tests were not carried out on CPC (also called no slump concrete) because it uses very low W/C. Table 3 presents a comparison between the properties of CPC and HPPC. Generally, CPC is a special type of gap-graded concrete, where the ﬁne aggregate is omitted entirely and a uniform size of coarse aggregate is used with low W/C. CPC needs to be properly com- pacted depending on the requirement of the application. As low W/C is used in CPC, it shows poor workability (<30%) and the spec- imens could not be cast properly. Therefore, it requires a good compaction for casting. It also needs proper curing, particularly; precast CPC products which are cured with steam or water to maintain the required strength. Steam curing mentioned in Table 3 was only meant to be used in manufacturing of precast porous concrete products. As shown in Table 3 and Fig. 1, HPPC exhibited better workability than that the CPC for the same void ratio. It did not require vibration for compaction and showed good cohesive- ness. This improvement in workability was due to the addition of appropriate amounts of SP and cohesive agents in optimum mixture proportions of HPPC. Table 4 shows the workability per- formance of CPC and HPPC. 3.2. Total void ratio JCI Test Method (Report on Eco-Concrete Committee for Void Ratio of Porous Concrete (draft)) was employed to determine the total void ratio of porous concrete cylinders ( u10 /C2 20 cm). Three specimens for each type of porous concrete were tested to calcu- late the mean value. The total void ratio was obtained by dividing the difference between the initial mass ( M1) of the cylinder spec- imen in the water and the ﬁnal mass ( M2) measured following air drying for 24 h with the specimen volume ( V), where as qM is the den

increased. In the range of 15–30% total void ratio, the coefﬁcient of permeability of all porous concretes increased linearly. 3.7. Strength development rate The respective specimens of CPC and HPPC were cured for 1, 3, 7, 14 and 28 days at 20 /C176C and 60% R. H. to examine their strength development rate. Fig. 7 represents the effect of curing period on compressive and ﬂexural strength development rates of CPC and HPPC. In general, the strengths development rates of CPC and HPPC increased gradually till a curing period of 14 days and became almost constant at a curing period of 28 days. HPPC showed higher strength development rate in compression and ﬂexural than CPC at the same curing period. The addition of appropriate amounts of cohesive agent and SP in cement paste signiﬁcantly affected the properties of HPPC. The ce- ment paste became cohesive with no bleeding or segregation and workable. The cohesiveness of cement paste successfully produced HPPC with lower total void ratio compared to CPC. The cement paste could also coat thoroughly each aggregate particle thus developing a strong bond between them in HPPC. 3.8. Method developed for self-compaction of HPPC A simple test method was developed and carried out to examine the self-compaction of HPPC. Fig. 8 demonstrates a diagram of a mold. Only two types of coarse aggregates of No. 6 (5–13 mm) and No. 7 (2.5–5 mm) were employed to cast HPPC specimens. Self-compaction of HPPC was carried out by freely dropping the porous concrete into a PVC pipe ( u10 /C2 100 cm) mold as shown in Fig. 8 . After de-molding, the tests carried out on these HPPC specimens were: slump and slump ﬂow, apparent density, com- paction index, total void ratio versus compressive strength and coefﬁcient of permeability, etc. All these properties were signiﬁ- cantly inﬂuenced by this method. Mixing of concrete was carried out at room temperature and the specimens were kept at that temperature for 1-day followed by 20 /C176C and 60% R. H. for 27 days. 3.8.1. Slump and slump ﬂow Table 5 presents the results of slump and slump-ﬂow of HPPC using No. 6 (5–13 mm) and No. 7 (2.5–5 mm) aggregates. The test data showed that their values are almost similar regardless of the aggregate size. It means that the aggregate size did not exhibit any effect on the workability of the HPPC. 3.8.2. Apparent density Apparent density was calculated by using the following equations: Apparent density ðg=cm3Þ¼ Mass of the specimen Volume of the specimen ð1Þ Table 6 exhibits the apparent density of HPPC specimens pre- pared by freely dropping method. It showed that the apparent den- sities of specimens were almost similar. This is due to good workability of HPPC that could ﬁll the voids of almost any size and shape. 3.8.3. Compaction index Compaction index was calculated by the following equation: Compaction index ð%Þ : ðD1=D2Þ/C2 100 ð2Þ where D1 and D2 are apparent densities of porous concrete placed by freely dropping it into a mold and according to JCI Method of Making Porous Concrete Specimens (draft), respectively. Fig. 9 represents the compaction index versus different posi- tions of HPPC specimens. The effect of cohesive agent on self-com- paction of HPPC is noticeable. Regardless of specimen’s position and aggregate size, the compaction index of specimens is more than 80%. This means that the HPPC showed good workability and cohesiveness. This is achieved by using appropriate amount of SP and cohesive agents in order to produce poro

3.8.4. Total void ratio and compressive strength Fig. 10 exhibits the effect of total void ratio on compressive strength of HPPC specimen taken from different positions. As dis- cussed above that the freely dropped mass of porous concrete itself has great inﬂuence on total void ratio and compressive strength. All porous concrete specimens taken from top most position to bottom position have total void ratio which ranged between 23 and 32%, respectively. These values are relatively larger than those of porous concrete specimens prepared according to JCI method. All specimens taken from top most to bottom position showed compressive strength higher than 10 MPa which is generally re- quired for applications in drainage pavement, stone protection and the production of precast porous concrete products. Normally, the compressive strength of HPPC is increased with decreasing total void ratio as seen in Fig. 10 . In fact, the total void ratio is affected by the addition of SP and thickening agent due to good workability achieved by the cohesive agent and the cement hydration products. They co-mingle and create two interpenetrat- ing matrices which work together, resulting in improved strength. However, the improvement in compressive strength is not only due to reduced porosity but it is also due to strong bond between cement paste and aggregate produced by the use of SP and thicken- ing agent at high total void ratio. HPPC can be produced according to desired strength and porosity depending on applications. 3.8.5. Coefﬁcient of permeability The permeability results of HPPC are presented in Fig. 11. It also shows the effects of self-compaction of HPPC due to cohesive agent and freely dropping method on permeability. Fig. 11 points out that all porous concrete specimens showed permeability values be- tween 0.9 and 3.1 cm/s which were acceptable as stone protection application. HPPC made with different size aggregates exhibited different permeability values. The aggregate gradation and speci- men’s position have a consistent inﬂuence on permeability of por- ous concretes. In general, all of these properties, mentioned in Section 3.8, are somewhat higher for HPPC layers taken from the bottom than of those specimens taken from the middle or the upper layers. These higher values are due to the good compaction provided by freely dropping of porous concrete mass. The compressive strength of HPPC specimens collected from the bottom to the top most layers is higher than 10 MPa which is generally required for stone protec- tion applications. In the applications of drainage pavement, making precast product and steep revetment (concrete block retaining wall) with porous concrete, the desired compressive strength is 10–12 MPa. In the application of steep revetment with porous con- crete, vibration or compaction cannot be done due the complexity of place. Therefore, HPPC can easily be used in such applications. The apparent density and compaction decreased with the use of the freely dropping porous concrete which eventually increased the total void. This means that large porosities or voids are devel- oped which caused a decrease in compressive strength of HPPC. However, the purpose of evaluating the self-compaction of HPPC is achieved by conducting this experiment. Therefore, it can be concluded that HPPC could be used effectively in protecting heavy Fig. 7. Effect of different curing period on compressive and ﬂexural strengths development rate. 10cm Steel P

stone and blocks of porous concrete along with river banks and water purifying systems without using any vibration equipment. It is to be noted that HPPC is a porous concrete and contains less cement binder and high amount of aggregate. Therefore, it must need some compaction by the tampering method regarding appli- cation or should be prepared according to the JCI Method of Mak- ing Porous Concrete Specimens (draft). Freely dropping method was considered to be an appropriate method to examine the self- compaction of HPPC considering ﬁeld applications such as stone protection and heavy stone revetment. All specimens had almost same apparent densities, i.e., HPPC showed enough workability to settle down in all type of shapes or spaces without applying compaction or vibration and gave more than 10 MPa compressive strength which is the requirement for ﬁeld application. CPC cannot be used in such application. 4. Conclusions Based on this laboratory study, the following conclusions can be drawn: (1) Use of combination of SP and cohesive agent could produce acceptable HPPC with good workability and strength properties. (2) The addition of cohesive agent to HPPC mixture could decrease the total void ratio and permeability and signiﬁ- cantly increase the compressive and ﬂexural strengths. (3) Regardless of types of porous concrete and aggregate size, results showed an almost linear relationship between the compressive strength and total void ratio, and between coef- ﬁcient of permeability and total void ratio for all porous con- cretes in the range of 15–30% total void ratio. (4) Apparent density, compaction, total void ratio, coefﬁcient of permeability and compressive strength of HPPC are signiﬁ- cantly inﬂuenced by developing new freely dropping method. The evaluation of workability of HPPC by this method is successfully determined. The apparent density, taken at different positions, is almost the same and showed compaction index more than 80%. The permeability of HPPC by freely dropping method is acceptable. However, HPPC when made with different aggregate sizes, exhibited differ- ent permeability values. Fig. 9. Compaction index versus specimen’s position. Fig. 10. Compressive strength and total void ratio versus specimen’s position. Note: /C3 , Total void ratio. Fig. 11. Effect of specimen’s position on coefﬁcient of permeability. 72 M. A.R. Bhutta et al. / Construction and Building Materials 31 (2012) 67–73

3.4. Relationship between total void ratio and compressive strength Fig. 4 represents the relationship between total void ratio and compressive strength of porous concrete. Regardless of types of porous concrete and aggregate size, the compressive strength of all porous concretes was decreased with a decrease in total void ra- tio. Porous concretes having the total void ratio of 15–30% showed an almost linear relationship between compressive strength and total void ratio. 3.5. Coefﬁcient of permeability Coefﬁcient of permeability of porous concrete cylinders (u10 /C2 20 cm) was determined in accordance with JCI Test Method for Permeability of Porous Concrete (draft). Fig. 5 exhibits the effect of aggregate size on coefﬁcient of per- meability of all porous concretes as well as the effect of cohesive agent on permeability of HPPC. It is evident from Fig. 5 that all the porous concretes showed permeability values between 0.25 and 3.3 cm/s, which were high enough to be used as a drainage layer for pavement structures or for porous concrete blocks. The aggregate gradation did show consistent inﬂuence on the perme- ability, i.e., the smaller the aggregate size, the lower is the perme- ability. Moreover, the effect of cohesive agent on permeability was similar to that on total void ratio of HPPC. Although the addition of cohesive agent could lead to a reduction in permeability, its value was comparable and acceptable with reference to the general requirement of the drainage. 3.6. Relationship between total void ratio and coefﬁcient of permeability Fig. 6 represents the relationship between total void ratio and coefﬁcient of permeability of porous concretes. Regardless of types of porous concrete and aggregate size, the coefﬁcient of permeabil- ity for all porous concretes has become larger as the total void ratio CPC HPPC Fig. 1. Bhutta et al. / Construction and Building Materials 31 (2012) 67–73 69

(5) The strength of HPPC is increased with decreasing total void ratio. The porosity or total void ratio is affected by the addi- tion of SP and thickening agent, however, the improvement in strength is not only due to reduced porosity but it is also due to strong bond between cement paste and aggregate produced by the use of SP and thickening agent. HPPC can be produced according to desired strength and porosity depending on applications. (6) HPPC is superior in strength and cohesive properties to CPC. It exhibited the ability to resist segregation, ease of compac- tion and suitable strength. It can be successfully employed in the applications of drainage pavement, stone protection and the production of precast porous concrete products.
